chunks.text: \chapter{Electronic Mail} {\it Information about e-mail is also available on-line if you have an account on an Instructional machine. See the file /usr/pub/email.help for more detailed current information. } \setcounter{mpfootnote}{0} \setcounter{f

ation. } \setcounter{mpfootnote}{0} \setcounter{footnote}{0} \section{Using Electronic Mail} There are various programs available for reading and sending electronic mail. The most popular is ``BSD mail''. (The ``mailx'' program on the HPs is basic

ail''. (The ``mailx'' program on the HPs is basically the same as BSD mail.)\index{\bf mailx} There are others such as /bin/mail, MH, elm, pine, Emacs mail, etc. We will only cover BSD mail and mailx in this guide. \subsection{Introduction to BSD m

x in this guide. \subsection{Introduction to BSD mail and mailx} This is an abbreviated introduction to {\bf mail} and {\bf mailx}. For the whole story type one of: \begin{quote} \% {\bf man mail} \\ \% {\bf man mailx}~~~~~~~~~~(on the HP systems)

\\ \% {\bf man mailx}~~~~~~~~~~(on the HP systems) \end{quote} The {\bf mail} program is composed of {\bf sending mail} and {\bf receiving mail}. To read any mail sent to you type one of: \begin{quote} \% {\bf mail} \\ \% {\bf mailx}~~~~~~~~~~(on

te} \% {\bf mail} \\ \% {\bf mailx}~~~~~~~~~~(on the HP systems) \end{quote} (If you get an error message such as {\tt Error encountered attempting to create lock file}, you should use the following command: {\bf mailx -f~~~\$MAIL}. See the sectio

command: {\bf mailx -f~~~\$MAIL}. See the section entitled ``Avoid problems with file locking'' in the /usr/pub/email.help file for more details.) You will enter the {\bf mail} program which has its own prompt, the ampersand (\&). (The {\bf mailx

s own prompt, the ampersand (\&). (The {\bf mailx} program uses a question mark (?) as the prompt.) All of the commands you can use to read, reply, and dispose of your mail are described in Section \ref{mail_com}. To send mail to other people, give

ref{mail_com}. To send mail to other people, give {\bf mail} an argument. To send \index{mail@{\bf mail} !sending} a message to one or more other people, {\bf mail} can be invoked with arguments which are the names of people to send to. You are the

h are the names of people to send to. You are then expected to type in your message, followed by an EOT (control-D) on a line by itself. For example, \begin{quote} \% mail cyndy@foo claire@bar \end{quote} There should not be any spaces in the mai

{quote} There should not be any spaces in the mail address, i.e., ``cyndy@foo'' not ``cyndy~~@~~foo''. The section below, labeled {\em ``Sending mail'',} describes some features of {\bf mail} available to help you compose your letter. {\bf Mail} ha

le to help you compose your letter. {\bf Mail} has a number of options which can be set in the {\bf .mailrc} \index{mailrc@{\tt .mailrc} file} file to alter its behavior; thus ``set askcc'' enables the ``askcc'' feature which automatically prompts y

he ``askcc'' feature which automatically prompts you to send a carbon copy. Section \ref{setting options} describes these commands. \subsubsection{Reading mail} \index{mail@{\bf mail} !reading} In normal usage {\bf mail} is given no arguments and

normal usage {\bf mail} is given no arguments and checks your mail out of the post office (located in /usr/spool/mail/username or /usr/mail/username), then prints out a one line header of each message there. You will have the mail prompt (the ``\&''

there. You will have the mail prompt (the ``\&'' or ``?'') at the left of the screen. The current message is initially the first message (numbered 1) and can be displayed by hitting RETURN or typing the message number. \subsubsection{Disposing of m

the message number. \subsubsection{Disposing of mail} \index{mail@{\bf mail} !disposing of} After examining a message you can {\bf delete (d)} the message. Deletion causes the {\bf mail} program to forget about the message. This is not irreversible

forget about the message. This is not irreversible; the message can be {\bf undeleted (u)} by giving its number, or the {\bf mail} session can be aborted by giving the {\bf exit (x)} command which exits the current mail-reading session and leaves al

ts the current mail-reading session and leaves all messages ``as they were'' in /usr/spool/mail/username (or /usr/mail/username on some systems) when you typed mail. Deleted messages will, however, usually disappear never to be seen again. \subsubs

sually disappear never to be seen again. \subsubsection{Replying to mail} \index{mail@{\bf mail} !replying to}\label{Rr} To answer a mail message type ({\bf R}) or ({\bf r}) after the mail prompt. WARNING: On DEC and HP systems, uppercase {\bf R }

RNING: On DEC and HP systems, uppercase {\bf R } replies only to the sender of the mail while lowercase {\bf r } replies to all people the mail was sent to so use it judiciously. On Sun systems, lowercase {\bf r } replies only to the sender of the

wercase {\bf r } replies only to the sender of the mail while uppercase {\bf R } replies to all people the mail was sent. Typing ``R'' or ``r'' will automatically repeat the To and Subject field (which can be altered with the use of a \~\space h comm

h can be altered with the use of a \~\space h command --- see ``Sending mail''). Text you then type in, up to an end-of-file (control-d), defines the contents of the message. Replying to mail messages uses the same commands as sending mail so all the

uses the same commands as sending mail so all the commands listed in the section {\em ``Sending mail''} apply to replying to mail. \subsubsection{Specifying messages} Commands such as {\bf print} and {\bf delete} can be given a list of message numb

d {\bf delete} can be given a list of message numbers as arguments to apply to a number of messages at once. Thus ``delete 1 2'' deletes messages 1 and 2, while ``delete 1--5'' deletes messages 1 through 5. The special name ``*'' addresses all messag

ugh 5. The special name ``*'' addresses all messages, and ``\$'' addresses the last message; thus the command {\bf top} which prints the first few lines of a message could be used in ``top *'' to print the first few lines of all messages. \subsubsec

t the first few lines of all messages. \subsubsection{Ending a mail processing session} \index{mail@{\bf mail} !exiting from} You can end a {\bf mail} session with the {\bf quit (q)} command. Messages which have been read get appended to your {\em

es which have been read get appended to your {\em mbox} \index{mbox} file unless they have been deleted in which case they are discarded. Unread messages go back to the post office (/usr/spool/mail/user or /usr/mail/username). The ``mbox'' file can

er or /usr/mail/username). The ``mbox'' file can become quite large if you routinely read messages but do not discard them. Typing \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf mail~~ -f} \end{quote} causes the {\bf mail} program to read in the conten

auses the {\bf mail} program to read in the contents of your {\em mbox} for processing just as if it were a current mail-editing session; when you {\bf quit ,} the {\bf mail} program writes undeleted messages back to this mbox file. \subsection{Ma

d messages back to this mbox file. \subsection{Mail Commands} \index{mail@{\bf mail} !reading} \label{mail_com} When you type \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf mail}~~~~~~~~~~or \\ {\tt \%}~~{\bf mailx} \end{quote} to enter the mail program there

ilx} \end{quote} to enter the mail program there are commands to use to manipulate and dispose of your mail messages. Each command is typed on a line by itself, and may take arguments following the command word. The command need not be typed in its

ommand word. The command need not be typed in its entirety --- the first command which matches the typed prefix is used. For commands which take message lists as arguments, if no message list is given, then the next message forward which satisfies t

n, then the next message forward which satisfies the command's requirements is used. If there are no messages forward of the current message, the search proceeds backwards, and if there are no good messages at all, {\bf mail} types {\tt No applicabl

essages at all, {\bf mail} types {\tt No applicable messages} and aborts the command. \begin{description} \index{mail@{\bf mail} !commands} \item [+] Goes to the next message and prints it out. \item [--] Goes to the previous message and prints it

m [--] Goes to the previous message and prints it out. If given a numeric argument {\em n,} goes to the {\em n-th} previous message and prints it. \item [?] Prints a brief summary of mail commands. \item [!] Executes the UNIX shell command which fol

item [!] Executes the UNIX shell command which follows. \item [Print (P)] Like {\bf print} but also prints out ignored header fields. See also {\bf print} and {\bf ignore.} \item [alias (a)] With no arguments, prints out all currently-defined aliases

rguments, prints out all currently-defined aliases. With one argument, prints out that alias. With more than one argument, creates a new or changes an on old alias. \item [chdir (c)] Changes the user's working directory to that specified, if given.

r's working directory to that specified, if given. If no directory is given, then changes to the user's login directory. \item [copy (co)] The {\bf copy} command does the same thing that {\bf save} does, except that it does not mark the messages it

oes, except that it does not mark the messages it is used on for deletion when you quit. \item [delete (d)] Takes a list of messages as argument and marks them all as deleted. Deleted messages will not be saved in {\em mbox ,} nor will they be avail

t be saved in {\em mbox ,} nor will they be available for most other commands. \item [dp (also dt)] Deletes the current message and prints the next message. If there is no next message, {\em mail} says ``at EOF.'' \item [exit (ex or x)] Effects a

says ``at EOF.'' \item [exit (ex or x)] Effects an immediate return to the Shell without modifying the user's system mailbox or the user's {\em mbox} file. \item [from (f)] Takes a list of messages and prints their message headers. \item [headers (

and prints their message headers. \item [headers (h)] Lists the current range of headers, which is an 18 message group. If a ``+'' argument is given, then the next 18 message group is printed, and if a ``--'' argument is given, the previous 18 messa

a ``--'' argument is given, the previous 18 message group is printed. \item [help] A synonym for ? \item [hold (ho)] also {\bf preserve} Takes a message list and marks each message therein to be saved in the user's system mailbox instead of in {\e

ved in the user's system mailbox instead of in {\em mbox .} Does not override the {\bf delete} command. \item [mail (m)] Takes as argument login names and distribution group names and sends mail to those people; basically, this is sending mail while

ose people; basically, this is sending mail while in the {\em mail} program. \item [mbox] Indicate that a list of messages you have read be sent to {\em mbox} in your home directory when you quit. This is the default action for messages if you do {\e

s is the default action for messages if you do {\em not} have the {\em hold} option set. This option would be set in your .mailrc file as ``set hold=/usr/spool/mail/user'' (or /usr/mail/user) where ``user'' is your login name; or ``set hold=filenam

user'' is your login name; or ``set hold=filename''. \item [next (n)] like {\bf + } or Carriage-Return) Goes to the next message in sequence and types it. With an argument list, types the next matching message. \item [preserve (pre)] A synonym fo

ching message. \item [preserve (pre)] A synonym for {\em hold .} \item [print (p)] Takes a message list and types out each message on the user's terminal. \item [quit (q)] Terminates the session, saving all undeleted, unsaved messages in the user's {

ng all undeleted, unsaved messages in the user's {\em mbox} file in his login directory, preserving all messages marked with {\bf hold} or {\bf preserve} or never referenced in his system mailbox, and removing all other messages from his system mailb

removing all other messages from his system mailbox. If new mail has arrived during the session, the message ``You have new mail'' is given. \item [reply (r or R)] Takes a message list and sends mail to the sender and all recipients of the specif

ail to the sender and all recipients of the specified message, or just to the sender. (see Section \ref{Rr}.) The default message must not be deleted. \item [respond] A synonym for {\em reply .} \item [retain] Add the list of header fields named (i.

m [retain] Add the list of header fields named (i.e., To From Date Subject, etc.) to the retained list. Only the header fields in the retain list are shown on your terminal when you print a message. All other header fields are suppressed. This gets r

ll other header fields are suppressed. This gets rid of all the extraneous information about machine hops the mail message took to get to you. The Type and Print commands can be used to print a message in its entirety. If retain is executed with no a

e in its entirety. If retain is executed with no arguments, it, lists the current set of retained fields. This is a useful option to set in your {\em .mailrc} file. \item [save (s)] Takes a message list and a filename and appends each message in turn

st and a filename and appends each message in turn to the end of the file. The filename in quotes, followed by the line count and character count is echoed on the user's terminal. {\em save} saves the file in the directory you called {\em mail} f

he file in the directory you called {\em mail} from; you can save a file in another director level by giving a full pathname. \item [set (se)] With no arguments, prints all variable values set in your {\em .mailrc} file. Otherwise, sets the option

ur {\em .mailrc} file. Otherwise, sets the option given in the argument. Arguments are of the form ``set option=value'' (no space before or after the equals sign =) or ``set option''. \item [top] Takes a message list and prints the top few lines of

kes a message list and prints the top few lines of each. The number of lines printed is controlled by the variable {\bf toplines} (which you can set in your .mailrc file) and defaults to five. \item [type (t)] A synonym for {\em print .} \item [unal

type (t)] A synonym for {\em print .} \item [unalias] Takes a list of names defined by alias commands and discards the remembered groups of users. The group names no longer have any significance. \item [undelete (u)] Takes a message list and marks e

em [undelete (u)] Takes a message list and marks each one as {\em not} being deleted. \item [write (w)] A synonym for {\em save .} \item [z (z-)] Will read the next screenfull (or back a screenfull) messages. Mail will only print the first 22--23 mes

essages. Mail will only print the first 22--23 messages, even if there are over that amount in your current mail. Typing {\bf z} will go to the next 24. Typing {\bf z-} will go back 24. \end{description} \subsection{Sending mail} \index{mail@{\bf m

ion} \subsection{Sending mail} \index{mail@{\bf mail} !sending} \label{sending mail} While you are composing a message, {\bf mail} treats lines beginning with the character `\~\space' specially. For instance, typing ``\~\space h'' (alone on a line)

instance, typing ``\~\space h'' (alone on a line) will repeat the message headers (To, Subject, cc). Other escapes will set up subject fields, add and delete recipients to the message and allow you to escape to an editor to revise the message or to

to escape to an editor to revise the message or to a shell to run some commands. Tilde escapes are only recognized at the beginning of lines. Some of these ``\~\space'' commands are more useful when applied to sending mail during a read mail sess

en applied to sending mail during a read mail session (such \~\space m and \~\space f). Here is a summary of the tilde escapes, which are used when composing messages to perform special functions: \index{mail@{\bf mail} !commands} \begin{descripti

index{mail@{\bf mail} !commands} \begin{description} \item [ \~\space b name] Add the given names to the list of carbon copy recipients but do not make the names visible in the Cc: line (``blind carbon copy). \item [ \~\space c name] Add the given

bon copy). \item [ \~\space c name] Add the given names to the list of carbon copy recipients. \item [ \~\space e] Invoke the text editor (ex) on the message collected so far. After the editing session is finished and you ``:wq'', you may continue

on is finished and you ``:wq'', you may continue appending text to the message. If \$EDITOR is set to another editor, then it will be used instead of ex. \item [ \~\space f messages] Read the named messages into the message being sent. If no message

essages into the message being sent. If no messages are specified, read in the current message. \item [ \~\space h] Edit the message header fields by typing each one in turn and allowing the user to append text to the end or modify the field by using

ppend text to the end or modify the field by using the current terminal erase and kill characters. \item [ \~\space m messages] Read the named messages into the message being sent, shifted right one tab. If no messages are specified, read the curren

ab. If no messages are specified, read the current message. \item [ \~\space p] Print out the message collected so far, prefaced by the message header fields. \item [ \~\space q] Abort the message being sent, copying the message to ``dead.letter'' i

ing sent, copying the message to ``dead.letter'' in your home directory if {\bf save} is set. If do not wish to have a copy of aborted letters saved then put ``set nosave'' in your .mailrc file. The \~\space q command is synonmous to typing control-C

~\space q command is synonmous to typing control-C twice. \item [ \~\space r filename] Read the named file into the message. \item [ \~\space t name] Add the given names to the direct recipient list. \item [ \~\space v] Invoke the visual editor on th

\item [ \~\space v] Invoke the visual editor on the message collected so far. By default, this is vi. If \$VISUAL is set to another editor, then it will be used instead. After you quit the editor, you may resume appending text to the end of your mes

u may resume appending text to the end of your message. \item [ \~\space w filename] Write the message onto the named file. \item [ \~\space dot] causes the {\bf mail } program to interpret a period alone on a line as the terminator of a message you

lone on a line as the terminator of a message you are sending (replaces control-d). \end{description} \subsection{Setting Options in mail} \index{mail@{\bf mail} !setting options} \label{setting options} Options are controlled via the {\bf set} an

ions} Options are controlled via the {\bf set} and {\bf unset} commands. Options may be set or not set, or set to a value. You can set them in your .mailrc file by doing: \begin{quote} set {\em option} or set {\em option=value} \end{quote} As an

tion} or set {\em option=value} \end{quote} As an example: ``set ask'' sets up the {\tt Subject:} prompt when sending mail (this is often set up for you by the system manager for all new accounts). Here is a list of options: \begin{description} \i

ere is a list of options: \begin{description} \item [append] Causes messages saved in {\em mbox} to be appended to the end rather than prepended. \item [ask] Causes {\em mail} to prompt you for the subject of each message you send. If you respond

subject of each message you send. If you respond with simply a newline, no subject field will be sent. \item [askcc] Causes you to be prompted for additional carbon copy recipients at the end of each message. Responding with a newline indicates you

message. Responding with a newline indicates your satisfaction with the current list. \item [autoprint] Causes the {\bf delete} command to behave like {\bf dp} -- thus, after deleting a message, the next one will be typed automatically. \item [esca

next one will be typed automatically. \item [escape] if defined, the first character of this option gives the character to use in the place of ``\~\space '' to denote escapes. \item [hold] This option is used to hold messages in the system mailbox

on is used to hold messages in the system mailbox by default. \item [metoo] Usually, when a group alias is expanded that contains the sender, the sender is removed from the expansion. Setting this option causes the sender to be included in the group

tion causes the sender to be included in the group. \item [nosave] Normally, when you abort a message with two interrupt characters (control-c's), {\em mail} copies the partial letter to the file ``dead.letter'' in your home directory. Setting the op

ad.letter'' in your home directory. Setting the option {\em nosave} prevents this. \item [crt] The valued option {\em crt} is used as a threshold to determine how long a message must be before {\em more} is used to read it, i.e., keep long messages f

re} is used to read it, i.e., keep long messages from quickly scrolling by on the screen. The usual setting is ``set crt=24''. \item [record] If defined, gives the pathname of the file used to record all outgoing mail. If not defined, then outgoing

all outgoing mail. If not defined, then outgoing mail is not so saved. Use is ``set record=mailsent'' (or any file name you wish). \item [toplines] If defined, gives the number of lines of a message to be printed out with the {\em top} command; nor

to be printed out with the {\em top} command; normally, the first five lines are printed. \end{description} \subsection{Using mail aliases} \index{mail@{\bf mail} !aliases} You can take advantage of the ``alias'' command in to define a single nam

of the ``alias'' command in to define a single name to expand to a list of names. For a personal alias define it in your .mailrc file: \begin{quote} alias cohorts bill jenny@ucb lee michelle \end{quote} Now when you type ``mail cohorts'' the mail l

te} Now when you type ``mail cohorts'' the mail list will be expanded to send mail to the everyone in the list. In mail you send, personal aliases will be expanded in mail sent to others so that they will be able to {\bf reply} to the recipients. Yo

will be able to {\bf reply} to the recipients. You can examine all of your aliases while you are reading mail by typing ``alias'' after the mail prompt. There is also a system-wide alias list. To read it type: \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf more /

ad it type: \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf more /usr/lib/aliases} \end{quote} \subsection{Introduction to Pine}\label{pine} \index{pine@{\bf pine}} {\bf Pine} is another program that you can use for mail. It is a full-screen editor and requires t

or mail. It is a full-screen editor and requires that your {\em terminal type} be set correctly. See Section \ref{termtype} for details. After your terminal type is set properly, you can start pine as follows: \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf pine}
as follows: \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf pine} \end{quote} You will see a menu like the following: {\tt \begin{minipage}{7in} \begin{verbatim} PINE 3.91 MAIN MENU Folder: INBOX 2 Messages ? HELP
: INBOX 2 Messages ? HELP - Get help using Pine C COMPOSE MESSAGE - Compose and send a message I FOLDER INDEX - View messages in current folder L FOLDER LIST - Sele
nt folder L FOLDER LIST - Select a folder to view A ADDRESS BOOK - Update address book S SETUP - Configure or update Pine Q QUIT - Exit the Pine program
QUIT - Exit the Pine program Copyright 1989-1994. PINE is a trademark of the University of Washington. [Folder "INBOX" opened with 2 messages] ? Help P PrevCmd R RelNotes

P PrevCmd R RelNotes O OTHER CMDS L [ListFldrs] N NextCmd K KBLock \end{verbatim} \end{minipage} } {\bf Pine} gives you a menu of commands at the bottom of each screen, so we won't provide a detailed li

of each screen, so we won't provide a detailed listing of commands in this manual. Please note that a command listed as \verb+^+C means hold down the \key{CTRL} key and type the letter ``{\bf c}''. \subsubsection{Ending the Pine Session} To exit {

\subsubsection{Ending the Pine Session} To exit {\bf pine}, just type {\bf q} ~ from the main menu. You'll be prompted with: {\tt Really quit pine?}. Type {\bf y} to confirm. If you are not at the main menu, you will need to type something to ge

e main menu, you will need to type something to get there. Most likely, this will be one of: {\bf m} or {\bf \verb+^+C}. You'll need to read the on-screen command options for the particular menu you're working with. \subsubsection{Help in Pine}

ou're working with. \subsubsection{Help in Pine} After starting the {\bf pine} program, you can type {\bf ?} and you'll see a menu of help topics like the following: {\tt \begin{minipage}{7in} \begin{verbatim} GENERAL INFORMATION ON THE
in{verbatim} GENERAL INFORMATION ON THE PINE MESSAGE SYSTEM Version 3.90 (built Wed Dec 28 11:20:41 PST 1994) University of Washington August, 1994 TABLE OF CONTENTS
August, 1994 TABLE OF CONTENTS (1) Introduction (2) Pine Help (3) Local Support Contacts (4) Giving Commands in Pine (5) Status Line (6) Main Menu Commands (7) Co
Line (6) Main Menu Commands (7) Command Line Options (8) Pine Configuration (9) Reading News (10) Reporting Problems -------------------- M Main Menu E Exit Help - PrevPage Y prYnt B
Help - PrevPage Y prYnt B Report Bug Spc NextPage Z Print All W WhereIs \end{verbatim} \end{minipage} } Just type the number of the topic you'd like help with. For more details, t

e topic you'd like help with. For more details, type {\bf man pine}. \subsubsection{Pico}\label{pico} \index{pico@{\bf pico}} If you invoke text editing mode in {\bf pine} (to compose a message, for example) the default editor you will be using is

example) the default editor you will be using is {\bf Pico}. It is similar to JOVE (See Section \ref{jove}.) Type {\bf man pico} for more details. \section{Network Mail: Mail Between U.C.B. and the Outside World} \index{network mail} \subsection{

Outside World} \index{network mail} \subsection{Accessing Networks} So far, we have described the message system as it operates within our local network. A network is a group of machines that are connected to each other. You may wish to send mes

connected to each other. You may wish to send messages outside our network to computers or hosts on other networks. U.C. Berkeley is directly connected to a network called the Internet. The Internet is a collection of regional networks that support

is a collection of regional networks that support the TCP/IP networking protocol. Over 250,000 computers are connected to this network. U.C. Berkeley can indirectly access networks like BITNET through computers that serve as gateways between the I

ugh computers that serve as gateways between the Internet and that network. There are very few networks that we cannot access in some fashion. \subsection{Sending Mail on Networks} Each network uses its own form of address. To send mail to users

es its own form of address. To send mail to users on other networks, follow the directions given previously in Sections~\ref{email:comp} and \ref{email:another} to initiate a message. Use one of the following forms in the {\bf To:} field: \begin{ver

following forms in the {\bf To:} field: \begin{verbatim} Internet username@host.organization.domain Internet username@host.subdomain.organization.domain BITNET username@bitnethost.bitnet \end{verbatim} If

username@bitnethost.bitnet \end{verbatim} If you don't know a network address, contact your correspondent (by phone or paper mail) and ask for a network address. You can send mail to network addresses on the Internet, BITNET and many other popu

resses on the Internet, BITNET and many other popular networks. \subsection{Mail to You Via a Network} \label{mailaddr} If someone wants to send you mail, they should use an address of the form: \begin{quote} {\em username@hostname.}{\bf EECS.ber

begin{quote} {\em username@hostname.}{\bf EECS.berkeley.edu} ~~~~~~~~~~ or\\ {\em username@hostname.}{\bf CS.berkeley.edu} ~~~~~~~~~~ or\\ {\em username@hostname.}{\bf berkeley.edu} \end{quote} where {\it username} is the username you use when you lo

{\it username} is the username you use when you login to your account and hostname is the name of the computer you are on. For an EECS Instructional account, the best address to use is {\em username@}{\bf cory.EECS.berkeley.edu}. \subsection{Connect

{\bf cory.EECS.berkeley.edu}. \subsection{Connections to Unreliable Networks} Network connections on the Internet and BITNET are reliable and do not often cause problems. However, other networks (especially networks like UUCP) can be unstable. Th

specially networks like UUCP) can be unstable. This means that sometimes your mail will not make it to another host or that it will travel slowly. The more complicated the network connection, the less likely it is that your message will reach its d

ss likely it is that your message will reach its destination. If you are experiencing a problem, you should address a question to {\tt postmaster@berkeley.edu}. Be sure to detail the problem and provide any error messages you might have received fr

vide any error messages you might have received from the remote host. \subsection{Poorly Rewritten {\bf From:} Fields} The {\bf From:} field produced by {\bf mh} or {\bf mail} may not always allow mailers on other hosts to reply correctly to your m

ailers on other hosts to reply correctly to your mail. Some remote hosts modify the address in the {\bf From:} field in an attempt to ensure an accurate reply. If the {\bf From:} field is changed incorrectly by a remote host, the address may be unu

correctly by a remote host, the address may be unusable. If this is a problem for one of your correspondents, you can insert a field called {\bf Reply-to:} that contains the appropriate reply address allowing users on other hosts to send you mail wi

allowing users on other hosts to send you mail with less aggravation. If the other mailer does not automatically use the {\bf Reply-to:} field, hopefully the other user may notice and try the return address listed there; {\bf mh} will not alter exp

address listed there; {\bf mh} will not alter explicitly stated {\bf Reply-to:} fields. You may also want to include a signature block that contains your name and network address. Signature blocks are a common way to end mail messages. To do this

are a common way to end mail messages. To do this, create a file called ``.signature''\index{signature@{\tt .signature} file} in your home directory and put your name and network address in it. This file will be appended to every mail message that y
file will be appended to every mail message that you send out.
